# Goblin Core

> Goblin Core is alpha, source-only C++23 server software that speaks the Redis RESP protocol and implements a focused subset centered on sorted sets (ZSETs) and hashes. It stores the same sorted-set data in about 51 bytes per member — roughly half of Redis 7.2.4, and about 35% under modern Redis 8.8 / Valkey 9.1, every engine measured on the same jemalloc 5.3.0 — and serves it faster; hashes get the same lean layout at about 45 bytes per field — roughly half of Redis 7.2.4, and 20–29% under modern Redis 8.8 / Valkey 9.1. This file is written for an operator installing, configuring, and running Goblin Core in production; it deliberately omits code internals.

Goblin Core listens on a TCP port and speaks RESP, so existing Redis clients and `redis-cli` connect unchanged. It supports `PING`, the sorted-set commands `ZADD`, `ZCARD`, `ZSCORE`, `ZRANGE`, `ZREVRANGE`, `ZRANK`, `ZREVRANK`, `ZREM`, and the hash commands `HSET`, `HSETNX`, `HGET`, `HMGET`, `HDEL`, `HGETALL`, `HKEYS`, `HVALS`, `HLEN`, `HEXISTS`, `HSTRLEN`, `HINCRBY`, plus admin commands `GOBLIN.MEMORY` (per-key memory introspection), `GOBLIN.OPTIMIZE` (in-place compaction), and `GOBLIN.SAVE`/`GOBLIN.LOAD` (point-in-time snapshots) — all of which cover both zsets and hashes. A key holds at most one type; a hash command on a zset key (or vice versa) returns the standard `WRONGTYPE` error. `GOBLIN.LOAD`/`--load` also import a Redis `dump.rdb` (Redis 2.6-7.2.x) to migrate sorted sets in (RDB hash import is not yet supported).

It is NOT a full Redis replacement. There is no automatic/background persistence, replication, clustering, pub/sub, Lua, transactions, ACLs/auth, modules, key eviction, or key types beyond sorted sets and hashes. Durability is via on-demand snapshots (`GOBLIN.SAVE` / `GOBLIN.LOAD` / `--load`), not per-write logging. Treat it as an in-memory, single-node sorted-set and hash service that you snapshot and reload, backed by a source of truth.

## Install and run

- Build from source (needs CMake >= 3.25 and a C++23 compiler such as GCC 14+/Clang 18+): `cmake -S . -B build-release -DCMAKE_BUILD_TYPE=Release && cmake --build build-release`. Optionally `cmake --install build-release --prefix /usr/local`.
- Run: `goblin-core --bind 0.0.0.0 --port 6379`. Defaults are bind `127.0.0.1` (localhost only) and port `6379`.
- Connect with any Redis client: `redis-cli -p 6379 ZADD leaders 42 alice`.

## Configure and operate

- Memory: budget about 51 bytes per member after `GOBLIN.OPTIMIZE` (about 35% under Redis 8.8 or Valkey 9.1, and roughly half of older Redis 7.2.4; measured with every engine on the same jemalloc 5.3.0). A single member is capped at 64 KiB.
- Hash memory: budget about 45 bytes per field (16-byte field + 16-byte value, flat across sizes) — roughly half of Redis 7.2.4, and about 20% under Redis 8.8 / 29% under Valkey 9.1, with smaller values widening the lead. Each field and value is capped at 64 KiB; keep larger values in a blob store (goblin-store.dev) with the key in the hash. On the quiet benchmark host Goblin Core leads every hash op (HSET/HGET/HGETALL) over the Redis family.
- Deployment pattern: bulk-load a set or hash, then run `GOBLIN.OPTIMIZE <key>` once before serving reads to compact it and repack the member/field index.
- Fragmentation: sorted sets and hashes auto-compact — when reclaimable arena bytes pass the live bytes (past a ~1 MiB floor) the structure rebuilds itself, so update/delete-heavy keys bound their own footprint. `GOBLIN.OPTIMIZE <key>` forces it immediately; `--zset-chunk-bytes`/`--hash-chunk-bytes` tune the per-type arena chunk (power of two; zset min 64 KiB, hash min 128 KiB). See llms-full.txt for every flag.
- Security: there is no authentication or TLS. Bind to a trusted interface only and put access control in front of it.
- Durability: snapshot-based, covering both zsets and hashes (hashes ride in their own snapshot section, backward compatible with older zset-only snapshots). Data lives in RAM; a crash loses writes made since the last `GOBLIN.SAVE`. Save on a schedule (and before planned shutdown), `--load` the snapshot on startup, and keep a source of truth for a full rebuild. `GOBLIN.SAVE` forks a copy-on-write child and returns immediately (`Background saving started`), so it does not pause the server; watch the log for completion. The common path is the default accelerated snapshot: a restart memcpy's the packed indexes back and loads about 5.7x faster than Redis (0.69s vs 3.98s to restore 9.5M members), with save about 5x faster. `GOBLIN.SAVE <path> NOACCEL` is the upgrade/migration path, not an everyday-restart option: a smaller, portable, canonical-only file for moving data across Goblin versions, architectures, or C++ standard libraries (where the accelerator would be rebuilt on load anyway).
- Migration from Redis: `--load /path/dump.rdb` (or `GOBLIN.LOAD`) imports sorted sets from a Redis RDB file (Redis 2.6-7.2.x). Other types are skipped — including hashes, since RDB hash import is not yet supported (move Goblin Core hashes with a native `GOBLIN.SAVE` snapshot); streams/modules abort. For Redis 7.4+ dumps, re-save under an older Redis or migrate over the wire (`SCAN` + `ZRANGE WITHSCORES` -> `ZADD`).

## Reference

- [Full operator guide (llms-full.txt)](https://github.com/adamdeprince/goblin-core/blob/main/llms-full.txt): self-contained install, configuration, every command and flag, and a production checklist.
- [README](https://github.com/adamdeprince/goblin-core/blob/main/README.md): build, run, command surface, and flags.
- [Benchmarks](https://github.com/adamdeprince/goblin-core/blob/main/BENCHMARKS.md): memory and throughput versus Redis, with methodology.
- [Performance and architecture overview](https://github.com/adamdeprince/goblin-core/blob/main/PERFORMANCE_BRIEF.md): where the memory and speed wins come from.
